title: Race-level pit-window distance features
summary: Strategy models describe optimal stint length as a function of pit loss and degradation, while real F1 race strategy varies by circuit, compound, traffic, and safety-car likelihood.
rationale: Strategy models describe optimal stint length as a function of pit loss and degradation, while real F1 race strategy varies by circuit, compound, traffic, and safety-car likelihood. Current runs use median TyreLife by Race/Compound but do not encode distance to likely stop windows.
implementation_hint: From train only, compute smoothed empirical PitNextLap rate by Year-Race-Compound-Stint-TyreLife and broader backoffs such as Race-Compound-TyreLife, Compound-Stint-TyreLife, and Year-Compound-TyreLife. Add features for posterior hazard, distance to local hazard peak, cumulative hazard within next 1/2/3 tyre-life bins, and whether TyreLife is inside the central 50/80% positive window. Use out-of-fold encoding for train and full-train encoding for test.
expected_effect: Can sharpen ranking around common one-stop/two-stop windows and add strategy priors that tree models may not infer from continuous interactions alone.
risk: High leakage risk if encodings are not out-of-fold for train. Sparse race-specific bins need Bayesian smoothing and fallback hierarchy.
